text:
Costco Samples - February 12th - Inglewood Ihop
(missing - idk - idk if i need it)
jk it’s here
I came here for the samples, but there don’t seem to be any. everyone told me there’d be samples, yet I don’t see them here.
And then I spot it. I’ve perused pretty much every kind of Reese/s there is - not trying them, mind you - just looking at them and thinking about how they’d taste. But I’ve never seen them put Caramel in it. Blasphemy is my first thought. After all the shapes - easter eggs and christmas trees, and large hearts for Valentine’s Day, they’ve remained loyal to the essentials - and that to me, is an exclusive relationship between chocolate and peanut butter. Some people say the ratios better in the big one's. But my brother insists that chocolate should have an edge over peanut butter.

But in this blasphemous creation - caramel is here, like a mistress, a sinner, the other woman. I’m itching to try one, knowing that I won’t like it - I’m not a big caramel guy on a good day, but the combo looks magical, the And because it’s costco - they come in these giant boxes. I’d be eating peanut butter cups for days. I know I’d start out strong, but by the the day, caramel would start to turn my stomach and on the 17th, I’d be begging to just trash the lot.

But this must be what they meant by samples. because one of the cases has a flap already opened. And I can just reach in and grab one. It’s better this way - now I get to try it, and not learn to despise caramel with my soul. I put it in my pocket because it would just fall through the slats of the carriage. Presently, one of the employees is strolling by with a gray and cart piled high with breakfast sandwiches. I wait until she has safely finished crossing. I am transfixed in watching her pass, and I wait till she is completely out of …, they had hovering inches from the pocket of my coat. Finally when I can take my eyes off the spectacle, I drop it in, and leave my hand in my pocket for …These places are always so air-conditioned.

March 18th - San Vicente Starbucks
How To Make An Art
→ making up games for yourself
→ towards paradox - ask yourself an unanswerable question
	→ a metaphor is competing patterns
@@PB@@
(pic)
@@PB@@
(top right corner: mh pic of two people agreeing with each other or looking in a mirror, agreeing with himself)

Internal Consistency
The most important aspect to get something accepted into the metaphor machine is 1 (/an advanced MM)
Set Theory is a way to conceptualize the process of making this happen.
If we are already aware of the AC we are trying to convey.
focus is the most important virtue, and refusing to get distracted by bells and whisltes. A desire for communication can go a long way in convincing us to stay on track in that our goal is to create the same concept in somebody else’s head, and with this goal in mind we can securely imagine the AC without getting too attached to any particular aspect of the procedure of expressing.

@@PB@@ However, there are situations where we are fully aware of the AC we are trying to convey. This is when “Set Theory” can come in handy, as it provides a way of conceptualizing and maintaining consistency between the patterns we are using to express (the unknown abstract concept). In short, Set Theory shows allows us to conceptualize/arrange patterns independent of the AC they are referring to.

SET THEORY
Let’s start simple with the game SET
@@PB@@
How to Make an A.R.T.
Systems of Movement
on the way to the station
there are two ways we can create ART
The second is “Synthetic ART” in which we
The natural way is similar to the scientific method. We have a question in mind, albeit one that cannot be answered, and we do “experiments” to reach our conclusion, all the while marking our progress - our data, as we go. these markings then become an expression of these experiments so others can take the journey with us.

@@PB@@
ART is often the result of accident - becoming obsessed with a question, and creating ART in the process. While obsession is generally unhealthy, we can purposely nurture our obsessive interests if we have the desire to create an ART.
The other way to create an ART] May be called conceptual art, in which we work backwards from a concept - we start from the concept itself, and then work to express it (rather than letting(?) the concept unveil itself gradually as we embark using the scientific /artistic process).

@@PB@@
Obsession - caught in the net
(pic)
One type of obsession is the result of a process in our brain going wrong, and that is the process of collecting patterns. We collect patterns to gain evidence, and eventually find the answer to a question, and (towards this end) collecting patterns is a finite process, which stops when the answer is gained. However, if we apply this process incorrectly - to something that does not have an answer, or that we do not have sufficient access to information that could lead us to the answer, or mind can get stuck in this in between state of collecting patterns and we will constantly have a line cast, searching in the depths for new evidence to finally answer our unanswerable question

and while this process would normally come to an end upon successful retrieval of the answer, when stuck in the throes of obsession the process is neverending, producing a stuck feeling while our fishing line stays permanently cast into the sea, with only false bites.
Incidentally, this unfortunate process can be manufactured for the sake of creating ART (or we can create art to end this process that has begun accidentally).

The Method
Collecting patterns is a normal process that will occur without our prompting; in order to induce this offshoot, we can be on the lookout for events that we note specifically
Rather than waiting for these emotional ? to pass so we can move from our heightened state of mind, we instead can grab a hold of them - grip them even more tightly, and ask ourselves questions
Often there is an emotional component to the things which get caught in our net; that we cannot stop thinking about…
connected to childhood trauma or romantic obsession. Also there is the intellectual obsession - a certain idea strikes a chord in our brain and we cannot lose it - something about the idea grabs ahold of us

about the subject that grips us, indulging in our usually repressed thoughts - imagining the scene to the end, rather than cutting it off in the interest of preserving our own mental health, ? to let ourselves spiral, or get on the train to crazy. Not: this indulgence should of course approached with caution, always in the context of the experiment, keeping one thought trained on the reminder that this is for the purpose of ART.
Rather than retreating, trying to get as close as possible to the emotional weight connected to this obsession, following the way your experiments make you feel. To understand this, you might remember the almost delicious feeling that comes with romantic depression - the feeling that though the feeling is bad, at least it is a real one. And even in wallowing in the despair of a breakup, we can allow ourselves to remember the select few ideas, images, or thoughts about our past beloved that triggers our regret in losing the person.

The Second Method: Game Theory - art by accident (Systems of Movement) Game Theory relies on the idea that within our mind is a “goal-based system” - a doughnut, if you will, hanging over our head, that we are constantly following. While the aforementioned doughnut may change over the course of the day, and our life, always, the act of following is the same - the desire to get somewhere, and the inability to do so as the goal post shifts based on our further position. Once we become aware of this situation, we may attempt to rid ourselves of the pursuit of the doughnut - ignore it, pretend we’ve reached it etc. But in this Method Two, we acknowledge and appreciate the existence of this doughnut, and enjoy, if only temporarily, the pleasure involved in trying to get to it.

To this end, the Second Method is about developing a game for ourselves
To do so, we can rely on the principles of game development, tightening and adjusting the strings to ensure our focus is laser focused on the doughnut and that we train our eyes only on this.
The addictiveness of video games and other types of games can be said to rely on our dopamine receptors, and the feedback system it creates in which anticipation of an event spikes the highest levels of happy excited chemicals.

This means that a certain level of challenge is especially enticing to use - and a goal that is “just out of reach” is the biggest incentive towards playing the game, and coming back to it. Counter-intuitively - this “just out of reach” feeling is the gaol, and the “goal” itself is only a “hanger on” that comes with exactly where our reach is - the “just out of” relationship between the goal doughnut and our reach is what is maintained, no matter where our reach is.
(and then go into the specific games I created and how they came about)

The Third Method - Conceptual Art So far, we have viewed the metaphor machine as a single direction operation, designed to extract meaning rather than create it. In this method, we go backwards through the machine, starting from the top down, so that we begin with the concept and then work towards expressing it. To do so, we must create an object that is sure to pass through the front gates of the metaphor machine, and to do so, we must become aware of the requirements for getting through said gates - by creating an object that appears to refer to abstraction, that was “made in mind,”

Systems of Movement
The formula
Imagine the process of making a paper mache bowl out of a balloon. First, we inflate the balloon, cover it with paper mache, then we remove the balloon - pop it. metaphysically, what role does the balloon play -d oes it exist, or is it a tool, only of the bowl.
This is the formula method - we create a thing so we can make a mold of it and then use that mold. The original thing is (?) - discarded. yet before the mold of the bowl exists, the balloon is very real, and in fact, is all that exists.
(pic)

Synthetic Art Using the mold of the other three methods, synthetic art produces a similar product - at least one that looks the same to an outside observer, but unlike the others, it is missing the essential concept - the balloon that keeps its shape - the Metaphor Machine sees the mold of the balloon, and assumes that the concept is there. In order to have this kind of A.R.T., we must replicate the effects of the balloon to produce the shape of the bowl with the goal of the Metaphor Machine recognizing it as such too. (And in this strange circumstance, the bowl allows us to imagine the balloon, though it never existed)

made in mind - tabs of the imagination thingamajig/placeholder/drawer mental blind spots we can create internal consistency without abstraction the use of “superficial patterns” Superficial Patterns - patterns that refer to nothing Systems of Movement Systems of Movement allow us to move when we are stuck. They are premade systems that we can apply to most any project and get from point A to point B - not for the purpose of getting there, but with the intent of accident - hoping that an accident happens along the way, causing a mutation, or for something to happen that we like.

And Game Theory, in this vein, is a slightly larger or more exact version in which we create a very specific “system of movement” for ourselves, within which within this the confines of this container, we create for ourselves, little moments of judgment, disguised by the game that they are a part of, so that gradually, we shape something intentionally (that also is influenced by accident). Note that game theory is a form of instrumentalism in that the game itself is designed to be a vehicle for these small moments of intent that appear periodically throughout the game.

@@PB@@ An example of this phenomenon Generally, the feature is used that by describing what object you would like to see, you can then create variations of said object, and fine tune them using the buttons of the program - ideally getting close to that which you imagined in your mind before beginning. However, I found that by using the prompt “six random things” in conjunction with some images and modifying words - I could produce six random images which could not be modified as well by the program, seeing as the individual images that had been created were smaller, less detailed, and (not modifiable, so that, even pressing the “variate” button would produce only a remnant of the original image, with a few features in common

@@PB@@
while the original program was quite enticing to me and took up quite a few hours of my time, this modified version called to me in my dreams, was the first thing I thought of in the mornings, and required a majority of my non-working time.
I can only speculate that the single “variate” command, coupled with the fun activity of poring over the six random images 24 created with each new variation, created an optimized version to access my pleasure - receptor system. Which is to say: the increased infrequency of finding a usable image upped its value, made its discovery all the more exciting for me. And I craved it more than anything.
Hopefully this allows you to understand the systems at play in creating a game, and to fine tune them as such.
Beyond this particular game,

@@PB@@ I came up with a fair few others that were similarly enticing, or at least, claimed as much of my attention for the period that they did. Of course, the games that I will soon describe are based on my particular proclivities, even brain design, and you will need to design games which fit your own proclivities - likely using larger, or more durable materials, if that is what appeals to you Dollar Designs Food Collages Pairings Special Little Pictures

@@PB@@
Another example of the formula in action (SOM) is referral. Now that we have learned about referral, both in regards to reading minds, and using art to refer to abstraction, we can pry our “bowl” from these “balloons,” and look at the concept of referral itself, and apply it to other things
We can see that the world is referring to something - is referring to god (or our mind?) (which is god…?) Patterns are the building blocks that refer to concepts, and the pattern of Synchronicty, or of coincidence, refers to god

@@PB@@ Animanimalism - Applied Instrumentalism I have ideas but I will not profess to know exactly we are so utterly unaware of our animal roots. Whether you believe in evolution or not, we have not evolved from animals - we are animals. And this dictates so many things about the way that we interact with the world on a subconscious level. Becoming aware of this is perhaps both deeply concerning, and allows for a new level of awareness - allows us to be more in control of actions, knowing, at least, from whence they stem. → The Systems in our brain - the faith spot etc.

(pic) @@PB@@ Set Theory Part IV Impossible thi In this exercise we place things in a wooden chest, to become a part of our “set.” These things should have one, and only one thing in common - each thing should refer to the abstract concept of the box, or follow the rules set up “by the container.” So far, we have understood the metaphor machine by its most basic function - the ability to “mold” objects or experiences - to extract the symbolic meaning from anything that counts as a symbol. But it is now time to make (?) understanding the intricate inner workings of the (?)

@@PB@@
Previously, we conceptualized a metaphor as a venn diagram, in which the interior ellipse is projected into the realm of abstraction.
Let us expand on this conceptualization so that we can make several (possibly infinite) comparisons, to look a bit like a daisy, where the central stamen now is the thing being referred to, also that which is projected, the abstraction
There are actually a few possibilities for how these outer petals might refer to the center; we will examine two.
In one possibility

@@PB@@
Making up a game for yourself allows you to always have something to come back to - a way of moving - a system or pattern of movement that can be used when you are stuck. When you are stuck, you can come back to this system
in conjunction with obsession, there must be a certain type - in pursuit of a paradox
you can cultivate the obsessive interest you feel in competing patterns

@@PB@@ If you have ever played the child’s classic telephone, then you’ll understand the subtle power of translation error. If the words passing from ear to ear are misheard the slightest bit, then this mistake compounds until the error is gargantuan, and the meaning is completely changed. For this reason, translation is a deceptively (simple/useful) system of movement. The whole intent of copying is to mimic exactly, and yet, it is these errors in mimicking that allow us to use this method as an agent of change. In a spinoff of the classic “procedure” relies on one of the primary requirements of the scientific experiment: repeatability. The Scientific Method calls for Scientists to document their procedure in such an exact way, that another scientist could repeat the experiment, and produce the same result. In this activity, we focus on the margin of error, and these small translation errors that can make the biggest difference - leading to an entirely different result.

@@PB@@
Using basic materials such as paper, tape, scissors and staples, player 1 creates an object while player 2 documents the steps involved in the creation, noting them on a sheet of paper. This paper is then passed to player 3, who replicates, as best they can, the creation of this object. Repeat as many times as desired, then compare the similarities and differences between the objects and what ambiguities in the written directions might’ve led to these “mistakes.”

@@PB@@
(”xerox”)
Take a somewhat simply image - a logo perhaps, and draw it out on a piece of paper. Give yourself 15-30  seconds to complete that, then cover up the original image.
Start with a fresh sheet of paper, and copy your drawing with an additional 15-30 seconds. Do this several times, and then compare the images. See how any inconsistencies become more pronounced as you go down the line of drawings. Does the drawing get simplifies as its repeated? What are the essential elements of the image that persist throughout all the copies?

Abstraction The word “abstraction” is used in so many contexts, we sometimes forget the basic meaning of the word. “To abstract” is to “simplify” - to essentialize - to take the most essential data points, and condense them, leaving out the extraneous noise. We are left with a version of the subject that allows us to see it for “what it truly is.” Inherent in the process of abstraction is a value judgment - what are the important bits of information, necessary to retain, and what can be considered extraneous, discardable? The decisions we make in abstraction reflect what we consider useful/important in the original subject and reflect our purposes in using the information. Abstraction is when the changes is a loss of information and it gets more and more essentialized - specifically, what we are trying to get to (taking out all the excess things that we don’t need)

We are constantly filtering information through the lens of trying to get to the kernel of meaning that is useful or pertains to us.
iteration
Iteration is the last system of movement, and it involves expanding on a singular theme or subject.
Iteration can be seen as a linear process if we are looking for the “best idea,” or it can be seen as an expansive process - simply coming up with as man ideas as possible. This expansive mindset is often encouraged in the context of a think tank, when we train our minds to value every idea equally - quantity over quality, with the understanding that this “non-judgement” zone allows us the freedom and flexibility to think outside “the box,” and come up with ideas we might not have otherwise.

Iteration is a process our brain is prepared to performa naturally, with the intent of thinking of a solution to a problem, and we can adapt this process in the name of seeking a plethora of ideas or solutions. Rather than discarding all but one, we can instead acknowledge all the iterations and appreciate them as a group.

Set Notation Set notation allows us to conceptualize a container within which {objects} which follow the rules/restrictions of the container may reside. This container is of course an abstract one, but in this exercise, we imagine it as a real wooden chest - open, and waiting for objects to be placed inside that will eventually come to define the logic of the container. The relationship between abstraction and thing that is being abstracted can be placed in various locations so as to provide the ideal set up for imagining the contents of this box in our mind. If we are to imagine the abstract rules which define the container of a set as surrounding, encompassing the objects within, then we are able to establish the correct relationship between abstraction and thing that is being abstracted.

This is almost as simple as knowing that an equation can only have one variable to be solved, and so making up a number to stand in for the other variable, in the meantime, thinking of the objects to fill the “variable,” and once we have all the objects imagined, remembering that this number was arbitrary, and using the new information of the objects imagined in the box to eventually define the container. In our imagination, the container is defined already - we simply can’t see what is written on the slip of paper placed between the two brass screws. And it is this belief that allows us to remember rather than imagine the contents of the box. Knowing the rules of the set exist in the label of the container, we have only to remind ourselves of the these objects which could logically fit within the wooden walls.

And again, the spatial placement of the box around its interior objects is correctly reminiscent of where the rules would go in relation to the objects within a set, which exists as a discipline one because it mimics the way our mind imagines things anyway. …the more real these rules seem to us, the more confident we can be in swing what can and cannot be inside the box.

In an ART piece, all patterns point back to the “interior object” - the concept of the piece, and in order to correctly manufacture these patterns, we must imagine this concept clearly. However, the process of making art is the process of imagining this concept, or coming to imagine this concept t- we use these patterns we manufacture to come to know the concept as something more than a feeling. Similarly, the objects in side the imaginary wooden box can be imagined because we know the box exists, and we think we know stuff about it. The process of inventing is a process of remembering, is actually a process of inventing. So far, we have discussed this as an exercise of the “imagination.” But in a last twist, we can procure a real wooden box, and create the objects within it in reality. Then we can label the box when we are done, finally knowing what set we were thinking about.

abstraction towards deletion Though it is not our ultimate goal, looking quickly back at art as an instrument of therapy, we can see the creation of ART as the opportunity for the systematic deletion of “unpleasant” feelings. Note first that often we cannot clearly define a feeling - it does not have a name, or we are too in the thick of it to call it out properly. We can use an exercise similar to the wooden box to understand our mysterious feelings, and the metaphor machine to delete them from existence. Rather than trying to pinpoint the feeling directly - use the “wooden box technique” to imagine things that are and are not the feeling. If things are too concrete, then colors can be used. Focus on visualizing the components of the feeling.

Within the metaphor machine exists an ambiguously faced disk, with room for various expressions of the abstraction to reside. These “expressions” can be considered features of the abstract concept, having one thing in common with an aspect of the abstraction - overlapping in one area, and one area one. Together, as a group, these analogies for the various aspects of the abstraction can gradually replace pieces of said abstraction so that what is left is nothing.

Competing Patterns in People “People as Paradoxes If we pay enough attention to the people around us, we are often faced with conflicting evidence about their character. Their observed actions point to directly contradictory information about the inner-workings of this person. There are two possible explanations. 1. One of the potential patterns is simply false - connected to something other than the quality we are studying. The other, more likely answer, is that there is a third explanation, combining the actions observed in both of

Each aspect of the abstraction is represented by one of the facets of the disk.
Systems of Movement
The Formula
Imagine the process of making a paper mache bowl out of a balloon. First, we inflate the balloon, cover it with paper mache, then we remove the balloon - pop it. Metaphysically, what role does the balloon play - does it exist, or is it a tool, only of the bowl.
This is the formula method - we create a thing so we can make a mold of it and then use that mold. The original thing is popped - discarded. Yet before the mold of the bowl exists, the balloon is very real, and in fact, is all that exists.
“Philosophy is Stupid” - March 23rd - Bodega Wine Bar
